Party_Details.Name: Hup Huat Food Industries (S) Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: Hup Huat Food Industries (S) Pte Ltd is a company incorporated in Singapore that carried on the business of importing and exporting biscuits, chocolate wafers and confectionery products packaged under the trademark 'Apollo'.
At the Annual General Meeting of the plaintiff held on 7 January 2002, the audited accounts for the financial year ending on 31 December 2000 were approved.
On 7 January 2002, the plaintiff held its Annual General Meeting attended by shareholders Tan Song Cheng, Tan Kok Guan, the Liang brothers and the company secretary.

On 9 January 2002, staff of the plaintiff, except for the accounts clerk Ling Sway Hwa, were informed of retrenchment with their last day of service stated as 28 February 2002, and three of them were re-employed on a temporary basis on 28 February 2002.
In January 2002, the plaintiff's business was reduced by 45% compared to the previous month.

In February 2002, the third defendant, using the name Taisan, imported Apollo products from AFI and exported them directly to the Middle East customers. At the same time, the Liang brothers, through the fourth defendant, arranged for the plaintiff's staff to load Apollo products into containers for Taisan in front of the fifth defendant's premises. The fifth defendant provided a forklift and warehousing space for Taisan.

On 1 February 2002, after receiving the draft minutes of the AGM, Tan Song Cheng wrote to the company secretary to state that he was not agreeable to the inclusion of the section under 'Other Business' because the issue was not included in the agenda for the AGM and was not put to a vote. He asserted that such an issue should be the subject of a special resolution at an Extraordinary General Meeting, the convening of which would require the board of directors' prior approval and the issuance of a proper notice with an agenda.
On 6 February 2002, the first defendant drew on the plaintiff's account an amount of US$49,800 and purchased 49 American Express travellers' cheques.

On 21 February 2002, Tan Song Cheng wrote to the company secretary requesting that an Extraordinary General Meeting be convened and sent a separate letter reminding the company secretary to reply to his earlier letter of 1 February 2002, copying both letters to the Liang brothers by fax and registered post.

On 15 March 2002, an Extraordinary General Meeting was held and Tan Kok Guan was appointed a director while Tan Song Cheng, his father, was appointed Executive Chairman and Finance Director. The Liang brothers tendered their resignation as directors, which was to take effect from 1 April 2002.
By March 2002, the plaintiff's export business had completely ceased. However, AFI continued to supply Apollo products to the order of the plaintiff until September 2002.

On 22 March 2002, Tan Song Cheng instructed his then solicitors to write to the Liang brothers to inform them that their resignation was not accepted as the company's affairs, which had been under their management, had not been properly resolved. Tan Song Cheng wanted various corporate matters taken care of by the Liang brothers before he would consider accepting their resignation, including disposal of all remaining stocks of the Company, resolution and/or termination of the lease of the Company's office and store premises at Hup Huat Food Industrial Building, and termination of employment of existing employees of the Company in accordance with the relevant labour legislations and regulations in Singapore.

On 22 March 2002, Tan Song Cheng asked the said solicitors to write to the company secretary about the draft minutes of the AGM. The letter to the company secretary enclosed a copy of the letter of the same date addressed to the Liang brothers and instructed that no statutory submissions in respect of their resignation be prepared. Referring to the draft minutes of the AGM of 7 January 2002 and to Tan Song Cheng's letters of 1 and 21 February 2002, the solicitors demanded a reply on the matters raised in those letters 'and/or a new draft minutes with appropriate amendments to address the matters raised'. The company secretary was also instructed to prepare the minutes of the recent EGM.

On 25 March 2002, the company secretary replied to Tan Song Cheng's solicitors stating that, with reference to the 28th Annual General Meeting held on 7 January 2002, the issue of 'closing down' the Company's export business, etc was brought up and discussed during the Meeting and Mr Tan Song Cheng, as Chairman of the Meeting, had not ruled the discussion as 'out of order'. The company secretary said it had therefore recorded the discussion under 'Other Business', but would act according to Mr Tan's instruction and remove the section, leaving him responsible to deal with any objection at the next meeting adopting the minutes.

In June 2002, Tan Song Cheng discovered that the Liang brothers' remuneration for the financial year ending on 31 December 2001 was $183,794. That was $154,435.44 above the authorised directors' remuneration.
In June 2002, Taisan started to use the premises of the Liang brothers at the Ang Mo Kio Industrial Park as its office with the brothers still having their own keys to the same.

In July 2002, the fifth defendant stopped purchasing Apollo products from the plaintiff and from 23 August 2002, began selling Apollo products using the plaintiff's trademark and the plaintiff's name as importer of the products.

In July 2002, the fifth defendant stopped purchasing Apollo products from the plaintiff and from 23 August 2002, began selling Apollo products using the plaintiff's trademark and the plaintiff's name as importer of the products.
By September 2002, the plaintiff's local business ceased altogether.

On 6 September 2002, upon the advice of the special accountant appointed for the company, Tan Song Cheng wrote to the company's auditors to reverse the sum of US$49,800 (S$91,134) from the sales discount account to the first defendant's directors' loan account. This reversal was done as a temporary measure.
On 10 October 2002, in reply to the auditors' request to confirm the amounts outstanding, the first defendant disputed that the amount of S$91,134 was due and owing from him to the company.
On 10 October 2002, in reply to the auditors' request to confirm the amounts outstanding, the first defendant disputed that the amount of S$91,134 was due and owing from him to the company.

In two circulars to shareholders issued by AFH in September 2001 and in October 2002 for the proposed renewal of the shareholders' mandate for recurrent related transactions of a revenue or trading nature, AFH and AFI stated they intended to continue manufacturing and/or supplying Apollo products to the plaintiff's order until October 2003.

In two circulars to shareholders issued by AFH in September 2001 and in October 2002 for the proposed renewal of the shareholders' mandate for recurrent related transactions of a revenue or trading nature, AFH and AFI stated they intended to continue manufacturing and/or supplying Apollo products to the plaintiff's order until October 2003.
Tan Song Cheng agreed that by the end of April 2000, the plaintiff no longer exported Apollo products to Indonesia as it was cheaper for AFI to export directly from Malaysia.
Another said he had been buying Apollo products from Malaysia instead of the plaintiff from about 1995. He had also exported such products to overseas customers.
Although the plaintiff had no real work for them since September 2002 when AFI stopped supplying Apollo products to the company, they remained in the plaintiff's employ.

The alleged profit of S$1.7 million made in 2001 by the plaintiff actually included other profits and income such as interest and profit from sale of its plant and equipment. The profit from sale of Apollo products was only about S$1.125 million. Out of this amount, the profit attributable to the local business and to the export business taken over by AFI must be deducted, resulting in profits of about S$600,000 and not S$1.7 million as alleged by the Tans.
In 1997, the plaintiff took a bank loan of more than S$8 million. This amount was then lent to the directors and shareholders with the plaintiff incurring substantial interest payments.
The Liang brothers stated that it was the common intention of the plaintiff's shareholders to wind down the business after the sale of the plaintiff's building in August 2000.
AFI had also taken over some of the plaintiff's direct export business since 1996.

The practice since 1972 was that the managing director would determine the salaries based on the preceding year's profitability. This was so even before 1998 when one of Tan Song Cheng's brothers was the plaintiff's managing director. The first defendant was appointed managing director in February 1998.
An audit confirmation dated 13 September 2002 indicating the amount in question was treated as a loan was sent to him subsequently.

On 16 August 2002, AFH, through the first defendant, announced that it was seeking a renewal of the mandate. The renewed mandate provided for a drastic reduction of the related party transactions between AFI and the plaintiff from RM12 million to RM3 million for the period between October 2002 and October 2003.

Around June 2002, the plaintiff was still carrying on the limited business of supplying Apollo products to customers in Singapore. AFI also had to continue purchasing machinery from the plaintiff and a mandate for this was also necessary.
In February 2003, the address of Hiang Li Engineering Pte Ltd still appeared as the address of Taisan in AFI's invoice to Taisan.

On 1 February 2002, the second defendant issued the first purchase orders for Taisan to AFI. These handwritten purchase orders had no address, no telephone or fax number and no authorised signature. The address used on AFI's invoices to Taisan was that of a company called Hiang Li Engineering Pte Ltd.
US$5,000 worth were encashed in the Middle East in May 2002.
18 of these were encashed in the casino on board a Star Cruise ship on 30 and 31 August and on 1 September 2002.

The special accountant appointed for the company testified that he started his work sometime in August 2002 and it took about two weeks. He drafted the letter of 6 September 2002 for Tan Song Cheng, who lived in Johor Baru, to sign.
Such was not mentioned in AFH's prospectus in 1996.
This was despite the company's letters informing the employees of the cessation of the activities of their departments with effect from 1 March 2002.
The plaintiff issued letters to its employees in early 2002 informing them of the cessation of activities of their departments with effect from 1 March 2002.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, based on the pre-2002 conduct and corporate arrangements, the plaintiff had an ongoing business and any exclusive distribution rights in Apollo products that could be affected by the defendants' subsequent conduct, and whether company funds recorded as sales discounts could be treated as loans to an individual director.
Party_Details.Rule: Company law principles govern how corporate decisions are made through shareholder meetings and board actions, how directors' remuneration and related party transactions are authorised and recorded, and how company funds and assets are classified in corporate accounts. Trademark and passing off law govern the scope of rights conferred by registration, the effect of consent to use by related entities, and the circumstances in which use of a mark on genuine goods and use of an importer's name on packaging can give rise to infringement or passing off.
Party_Details.Application: The plaintiff's historical role as importer and exporter of Apollo products, its shareholding and board structure, its financial practices regarding directors' remuneration and use of travellers' cheques, and its communications and resolutions around January to March 2002 define the baseline of its business and rights before the dispute. The absence of a written distributorship agreement, the existence of related party transactions within the Apollo group, and the practice of allowing AFI to manufacture and sell Apollo products in multiple markets frame the scope of any distribution and trademark-related interests. The recording and later reclassification of the US$49,800 transaction in the plaintiff's accounts, together with the timing of encashment of travellers' cheques, provide the factual basis for assessing how that transaction should be treated in relation to the plaintiff's assets.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court analysed whether the plaintiff had in fact decided to close down its business at the AGM on 7 January 2002, relying on the contemporaneous draft minutes, the conduct of the parties, and the informal manner in which the company had historically been run. It concluded that the shareholders had assented to closing down the business and that the plaintiff could not later characterise subsequent business by others as diversion. The court also examined whether there was any exclusive distributorship agreement between AFI and the plaintiff, noting the absence of written terms, the content of AFH's prospectus and circulars, and evidence that other parties had imported and exported Apollo products. It held that no exclusive distributorship agreement existed. On directors' remuneration, the court considered Article 95 of the Articles of Association, the long-standing practice of setting remuneration informally by the managing director, and the level of profits, and determined that the remuneration paid to the Liang brothers was consistent with past practice and not commercially indefensible. Regarding the US$49,800 travellers' cheques, the court evaluated the timing of the special accountant's work, the letter of 6 September 2002, the accounts clerk's evidence, and the first defendant's explanation about commissions, and found that misappropriation was not proved on a balance of probabilities, treating the amount as a loan to be repaid when loans were recalled. For the fifth defendant, the court found that it lacked the requisite knowledge for conspiracy or dishonest assistance and that its conduct in allowing loading, unloading, and storage did not render it responsible for any diversion, particularly since the plaintiff's business had been assented to be closed. On trademark and passing off, the court held that the plaintiff's trademark rights were limited by its consent to AFI's manufacture and sale of Apollo products, that the fifth defendant's parallel importation of genuine goods fell within the statutory defence under section 29(1) of the Trade Marks Act, and that the plaintiff had not established a separate local goodwill as importer or any loss from confusion. The court therefore dismissed all of the plaintiff's claims against all defendants except for the admitted monetary claim of S$158,331.75 against the fifth defendant, for which judgment had already been entered with interest and costs.

Party_Details.Name: Liang Chiang Heng

Facts.Text: The first defendant, Liang Chiang Heng, is a minority shareholder of the plaintiff and, together with his brother, holds 44.4% of the plaintiff's shares.
Until 31 March 2002, the first defendant was a director of the plaintiff.
The first defendant is the managing director and chairman of the board of directors of Apollo Food Industries (M) Sdn Bhd and Apollo Food Holdings Berhad.

Through their shareholding in Keynote Capital Sdn Bhd, the first defendant and his brother controlled 50.29% of Keynote Capital, which in turn controlled 50.82% of the shares in Apollo Food Holdings Berhad.
On 9 January 2002, the first defendant and his brother initiated retrenchment of all staff of the plaintiff except for the accounts clerk, with the last day of service stated as 28 February 2002.
On 6 February 2002, the first defendant drew US$49,800 from the plaintiff's bank account and purchased 49 American Express travellers' cheques.
In May 2002, the first defendant encashed US$5,000 worth of the American Express travellers' cheques in the Middle East.
On 30 and 31 August and 1 September 2002, the first defendant encashed 18 of the American Express travellers' cheques in the casino on board a Star Cruise ship.
At the AGM on 7 January 2002, the first defendant raised the issue of closing down the plaintiff's export business and mentioned allowing other dealers to handle the local business.
At the AGM on 7 January 2002, the first defendant asked shareholder Tan Song Cheng whether he would like to take over the plaintiff's business.
After the AGM on 7 January 2002, the first defendant instructed the fourth defendant, then an employee of the plaintiff, to issue letters of termination of employment to the plaintiff's staff.

After the January 2002 AGM, the first defendant consulted an independent director of Apollo Food Holdings Berhad and the head of the internal audit team for AFH and AFI about AFI exporting directly to overseas customers previously served by the plaintiff.

In early 2002, acting through AFI, the first defendant decided that AFI would give the Middle East export business for Apollo products to Taisan as a temporary arrangement while AFI took over exports to other countries.

At the EGM on 15 March 2002, the first defendant, together with his brother, tendered his resignation as a director of the plaintiff with effect from 1 April 2002 and stated that he would continue to go to the office daily and perform the same duties until the company was wound up.

In June 2002, the first defendant arranged for Taisan to use part of premises at Ang Mo Kio Industrial Park for S$3,000 per month, which premises were also used by AFI to receive machine parts and meet customers.
Before Taisan had its own premises in 2002, the first defendant allowed the fourth defendant to use the fax machine at his home for Taisan's business.
In 2002, the first defendant allowed the fourth defendant to use his spare car and provided a mobile phone for Taisan's business, with Taisan bearing the operating expenses.

Around April 2002, during a visit to the Middle East, the first defendant learned from customers that Taisan was exporting Apollo products through Hup Huat Import & Export and thereafter allowed that arrangement to continue.
For the financial year ending 31 December 2000, the first defendant proposed directors' remuneration of S$176,363 based on the plaintiff's profit of about S$1,475,000 in the preceding year.
The first defendant was appointed managing director of the plaintiff in February 1998.
In February 2002, the first defendant instructed that the US$49,800 withdrawn from the plaintiff's account be entered in the company's books as discounts for export debtors.
In October 2002, the first defendant responded to an audit confirmation request disputing that S$91,134 was due from him to the plaintiff.
Around late August 2002, the first defendant encashed the remaining American Express travellers' cheques after being informed that the amount had been treated as a loan in the plaintiff's accounts.

Before 2002, the first defendant made numerous trips to the Middle East in connection with Apollo products and used travellers' cheques and cash withdrawn from the plaintiff's funds for payments there.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, given the plaintiff's corporate decisions and practices, the first defendant's actions in initiating staff retrenchment, reallocating export business, arranging facilities for Taisan, determining remuneration, and withdrawing and using company funds via travellers' cheques were consistent with his roles in the group and the plaintiff's business structure.
Party_Details.Rule: Directors' conduct is assessed against corporate decisions made through shareholder and board processes, established company practices on remuneration and use of funds, and the framework governing related party transactions within corporate groups. Transactions recorded in company accounts as discounts or loans are evaluated based on their documentation, timing, and the surrounding corporate instructions.
Party_Details.Application: The first defendant's positions in the plaintiff and in the Apollo group, his involvement in the January and March 2002 meetings, and his subsequent steps to retrench staff, terminate leases, and reallocate export business to AFI and Taisan align with the plaintiff's shift away from operating its own export business. His role in setting remuneration and directing the recording of the US$49,800 transaction as discounts, later treated as a loan, is framed by the plaintiff's long-standing informal remuneration practices and the absence of contemporaneous formal resolutions on that specific transaction.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court accepted the first defendant's account of the AGM discussions and subsequent conduct, finding that the shareholders had agreed to close down the plaintiff's business and that the first defendant's actions in retrenching staff, terminating leases, and reallocating business were consistent with that decision. It held that there was no diversion of business because the plaintiff had decided to cease operations. On remuneration, the court found that the amount paid was in line with historical practice and proportionate to profits, and that it would be inequitable to apply Article 95 retrospectively. Regarding the US$49,800 travellers' cheques, the court concluded that misappropriation was not proved, noting the timing of the special accountant's advice, the possibility of error in the accounts clerk's recollection, and the first defendant's explanation that the funds were for commissions, and treated the amount as a loan to be repaid when loans were recalled.

Party_Details.Name: Liang Kim Poh

Facts.Text: The second defendant, Liang Kim Poh, is a minority shareholder of the plaintiff and, together with his brother, holds 44.4% of the plaintiff's shares.
Until 31 March 2002, the second defendant was a director of the plaintiff.
The second defendant is an executive director of Apollo Food Industries (M) Sdn Bhd and Apollo Food Holdings Berhad and is in charge of both local and export sales.

On 9 January 2002, the second defendant, together with his brother, began retrenching all staff of the plaintiff except for the accounts clerk, with the last day of service stated as 28 February 2002.
On 1 February 2002, the second defendant issued the first handwritten purchase orders from Taisan to AFI without address, telephone or fax number, or authorised signature.

In February 2002, the second defendant arranged for the plaintiff's staff to load Apollo products into containers for Taisan in front of the fifth defendant's premises, with the fifth defendant providing a forklift and warehousing space.

Between February and July 2002, the second defendant enlisted the help of the fifth defendant to allow Taisan to store Apollo products in the fifth defendant's warehouse and to use its forklift for loading and unloading.
In 2002, the second defendant allowed the fourth defendant to use his spare car and provided a mobile phone for Taisan's business, with Taisan bearing the operating expenses.

In 2002, the second defendant took orders for goods from Taisan over the telephone when orders were urgent, and did the same for other regular customers without requiring immediate written purchase orders.
In 2002, the second defendant signed invoices to goods had been received by customers when customers forgot to sign, sometimes in the presence of Tan Song Cheng's children working in AFI.
In early 2002, the second defendant, on behalf of AFI, approved a credit facility of US$150,000 for 30 days for Taisan so that Taisan would not have to obtain bank financing.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the second defendant's actions in issuing purchase orders for Taisan, arranging use of the plaintiff's staff and the fifth defendant's facilities, providing resources to the fourth defendant, approving credit for Taisan, and transferring local business were consistent with the plaintiff's corporate decisions and the structure of the Apollo group's distribution.
Party_Details.Rule: Directors' and senior executives' conduct in reallocating business and extending facilities to third parties is evaluated in light of corporate decisions, established business practices, and the framework for related party and credit transactions within a corporate group.
Party_Details.Application: The second defendant's role in sales for AFI and AFH, his participation in staff retrenchment and business reallocation after the January 2002 AGM, and his operational support to Taisan and the fifth defendant reflect the practical implementation of the shift of export and local distribution away from the plaintiff. His approval of credit facilities and use of shared resources are situated within the ongoing commercial relationships among AFI, the plaintiff, Taisan, and the fifth defendant.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court treated the second defendant's conduct as part of the broader implementation of the decision to close down the plaintiff's business and reallocate distribution functions. It found no diversion of business from the plaintiff because the plaintiff had agreed to cease operations, and therefore rejected claims that the second defendant's assistance to Taisan and the fifth defendant amounted to conspiracy or dishonest assistance.

Party_Details.Name: Koh Tong Chye Jenson trading as Taisan Import & Export

Facts.Text: The third defendant, Koh Tong Chye Jenson, is the nephew of the Liang brothers and the grand nephew of Tan Song Cheng.
On 14 January 2002, the third defendant set up Taisan Import and Export as a sole proprietorship and was its sole proprietor until 26 March 2002.
In December 2001, the third defendant set up Hup Huat Import & Export as a separate business.
The third defendant was an employee of the plaintiff between 1988 and 1991.
In early 2002, the third defendant used the address of Hiang Li Engineering Pte Ltd as Taisan's address on AFI's invoices.

After setting up Taisan in January 2002, the third defendant approached the first defendant, as chairman and managing director of AFI, to obtain a distributorship of Apollo products after the plaintiff decided to close down its export business.
The third defendant asked the fourth defendant to assist him with import and export documentation for Taisan's business.
Profits made by Taisan during the period when the third defendant was proprietor were declared in his income tax returns.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the third defendant's establishment and operation of Taisan and Hup Huat Import & Export, and his dealings in Apollo products, intersected with any ongoing business or rights of the plaintiff in a way that could affect liability.
Party_Details.Rule: Liability of a third-party trader is assessed in light of existing corporate decisions, distribution structures, and the trader's relationships with group companies and their directors, without presuming obligations beyond those arising from observable arrangements.
Party_Details.Application: The third defendant's creation of Taisan and Hup Huat Import & Export, his prior employment with the plaintiff, and his approach to AFI for a distributorship occurred after the plaintiff's internal discussions about closing its export business. His use of AFI as supplier and his export of Apollo products to the Middle East fit within AFI's broader move to handle exports through entities other than the plaintiff.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court found that the plaintiff had decided to close down its business and that there was no exclusive distributorship agreement. It held that appointing and assisting Taisan to handle Middle East exports did not divert existing business from the plaintiff. The court also found no evidence that the Liang brothers had a beneficial interest in Taisan or Hup Huat Import & Export that would give rise to liability in this action.

Party_Details.Name: Tan Kwang Heng trading as Taisan Import & Export

Facts.Text: The fourth defendant, Tan Kwang Heng, became the sole proprietor of Taisan Import and Export with effect from 27 March 2002.
The fourth defendant had worked for the plaintiff for about 29 years, dealing with import and export documentation.
After the AGM of 7 January 2002, the fourth defendant, then an employee of the plaintiff, was instructed by the first defendant to issue letters of termination of employment to the plaintiff's staff.
In February 2002, while still employed by the plaintiff, the fourth defendant worked for Taisan at night at home and delivered Taisan's purchase orders to the second defendant's residence.
In early 2002, the fourth defendant used the fax machine at the first defendant's home for Taisan's business before Taisan had its own premises.

From 27 March 2002, as sole proprietor of Taisan, the fourth defendant took over the import of Apollo products and continued to assist the third defendant with export documentation for Hup Huat Import & Export.
In June 2002, Taisan began using premises at Ang Mo Kio Industrial Park associated with the Liang brothers as its office, with the brothers retaining keys.

In 2002, after being retrenched by the plaintiff, the fourth defendant gave up the car provided by the plaintiff and used a spare car and mobile phone provided by the second defendant for Taisan's business, with Taisan paying the operating expenses.
Profits made by Taisan after 27 March 2002 were declared in the fourth defendant's income tax returns.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the fourth defendant's transition from employee of the plaintiff to proprietor of Taisan, and his handling of import and export documentation for Apollo products, intersected with any ongoing business or rights of the plaintiff in a way that could affect liability.
Party_Details.Rule: An individual's liability for operating a new business is assessed in light of the timing of corporate decisions of the former employer, the nature of the new business's relationship with group companies, and the absence or presence of ongoing contractual rights of the former employer.
Party_Details.Application: The fourth defendant's long experience with the plaintiff's import and export documentation, his involvement in issuing termination letters after the January 2002 AGM, and his subsequent operation of Taisan and assistance to Hup Huat Import & Export occurred against the backdrop of the plaintiff's move to cease its export business and AFI's decision to channel exports through other entities.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court viewed the fourth defendant as a trusted former employee engaged by Taisan to handle documentation and logistics for Apollo exports after the plaintiff had decided to close down its business. It found no diversion of existing business from the plaintiff and no basis to hold the fourth defendant liable for conspiracy or dishonest assistance.

Party_Details.Name: Ban Hock Trading Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: The fifth defendant, Ban Hock Trading Pte Ltd, is a company incorporated in Singapore carrying on the business of wholesalers in confectionery products, including Apollo products.
Since about 1984, the fifth defendant had purchased Apollo products from the plaintiff for resale, including export to Brunei.
Between February and July 2002, the fifth defendant allowed Taisan to store Apollo products in its warehouse and to use its forklift for loading and unloading as a favour to the second defendant.
In 2002, loading and unloading of Apollo products for Taisan took place along the public road in front of the fifth defendant's premises.
In July 2002, the fifth defendant stopped purchasing Apollo products from the plaintiff and began purchasing Apollo products directly from AFI.
Between July and October 2002, the fifth defendant sold Apollo products that bore the plaintiff's name as importer, which were stocks previously purchased from the plaintiff.

After the issue of the plaintiff's name appearing as importer on Apollo products was brought to its attention, the fifth defendant ensured that goods offered for sale no longer bore the plaintiff's name as importer.
In its dealings with both the plaintiff and AFI, the contact person was the second defendant and it made no difference to the fifth defendant whether it was dealing with one or the other.
Since 2002, AFI had been dealing directly with the fifth defendant and others in Singapore.
The fifth defendant obtained better prices for Apollo products when purchasing directly from AFI compared to purchasing from the plaintiff.
Before February 2002, the plaintiff had also used the warehouse area near the fifth defendant's premises in Woodlands as a convenient spot for loading and unloading Apollo products.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the fifth defendant's storage, handling, and sale of Apollo products, including products bearing the plaintiff's name as importer, and its direct purchases from AFI, affected any rights of the plaintiff in relation to diversion of business, trademark infringement, or passing off.
Party_Details.Rule: A reseller's liability for diversion, trademark infringement, or passing off is assessed based on its knowledge of corporate relationships, the nature of its sourcing arrangements, the authenticity of the goods, the presence of consent from the trademark proprietor or related entities, and whether any separate goodwill attaches to the importer distinct from the product brand.
Party_Details.Application: The fifth defendant's long-standing role as a purchaser of Apollo products, its later direct sourcing from AFI at better prices, and its temporary storage and handling of goods for Taisan occurred while AFI was supplying genuine Apollo products with the plaintiff's consent. Its sale of products bearing the plaintiff's name as importer involved stock originally purchased from the plaintiff, and its subsequent removal of the plaintiff's name from goods after the issue was raised shows how it adjusted its conduct once concerns were communicated.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court held that the fifth defendant had no sufficient knowledge of the internal relationship between the plaintiff and AFI or of any duties owed by the Liang brothers to support claims of conspiracy or dishonest assistance, and that its assistance in storage and loading did not amount to diversion of business because the plaintiff had decided to close down its business. On trademark infringement, the court found that the fifth defendant was importing genuine Apollo products from AFI and could rely on the statutory defence under section 29(1) of the Trade Marks Act, as the plaintiff had consented to AFI putting the goods on the market. On passing off, the court concluded that the plaintiff had not established a separate local goodwill as importer distinct from the goodwill in the Apollo brand, that traders purchased Apollo products because of the brand rather than the identity of the importer, and that no loss from confusion was proved. The only liability of the fifth defendant in this action was for the admitted monetary claim of S$158,331.75, for which judgment had been entered with interest and costs, while all other claims against it were dismissed.
